message:
fix(writing): fresh-eyes corrections to recently-added copy

Six real issues caught by a read-through:

1. Exchange 1 (stale beneficiary form) had the ERISA default
   backwards. For a 401(k), federal law makes the current spouse
   the default beneficiary unless they signed a spousal-consent
   waiver naming someone else. The prior copy implied the ex-wife
   would get the money by default, which is only true for non-ERISA
   accounts (e.g. IRAs). Reframed the exchange to cover retirement
   accounts generally and correctly distinguishes the IRA case
   (whatever the form says, full stop) from the 401(k) case (current
   spouse has some federal-law default protection, depends on
   whether the consent was ever signed).

2. Exchange 2 (blended-family default) overclaimed QTIP trust
   mechanics. QTIPs require income to the surviving spouse but
   principal access is an optional drafting choice, not a default.
   Dropped the principal-access clause; kept the income-for-life
   description that is the defining feature.

3. Exchange 2 also said the disinherited kids "have nothing they
   can do about it legally." Softened to "have very limited
   recourse" — there are narrow state-law remedies in some
   jurisdictions; the original phrasing was too categorical.

4. Privacy FAQ claimed transcripts "are not used to train the
   models by default" on consumer tiers for both providers. That
   is true for Anthropic Claude Max/Pro but has historically not
   been true for OpenAI's consumer plans (opt-out is required via
   privacy settings). Rewrote to describe each provider's default
   accurately and to point users at their account's privacy
   settings.

5. Tips bullet claimed the skill "tracks what it is still waiting
   on." That overstates a documented skill capability; softened to
   "notes what is still unanswered and will revisit it when you
   come back," which is honest about what natural in-session memory
   plus the intake-record provide.

6. Privilege FAQ opening said "you do not have an attorney-client
   relationship with an AI chatbot, so there is no privilege to
   waive in the conventional sense." That opener could be misread
   as "no concern." Smoothed into "AI chatbots are not attorneys,
   so conversations with them are not themselves privileged, and
   case law has held this can forfeit privilege on the same
   subject in a later conversation with a real lawyer" — same
   substance, less easy to quote out of context.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

You: When I started the job, so... 2009? I think I put my ex-wife - down at the time. That was before I met Sam. + down on the IRA. That was before I met Sam.

Agent: That is a - landmine; the plan administrator will pay your ex unless - you and Sam signed a waiver naming someone else. It does - not matter what your will says. We are flagging this as - a first-action item in the Attorney Engagement Brief so - it is the first thing your lawyer fixes. + landmine. Beneficiary forms override your will for + retirement accounts, and whoever is named on the form at + the time of your death is who gets the money. For the + IRA, it is whatever the form says, full stop. The 401(k) + has some federal-law protection for your current spouse + by default, but the details depend on whether Sam ever + signed a spousal consent. Every retirement form needs to + be re-verified; we are flagging this as a first-action + item in the Attorney Engagement Brief so it is the first + thing your lawyer fixes.

Today, it has not. + Short answer: AI chatbots are not attorneys, so + conversations with them are not themselves privileged, and + at least one recent New York case has held that discussing + otherwise-privileged legal matters with a consumer-grade AI + can forfeit the privilege you would have had in a + conversation with a human lawyer on the same subject. The + law here is new and under-developed, and I personally + think it is outrageous that a human lawyer gets privilege + on the same conversation an AI does not; that should + change. Today, it has not.
